summary:
Chemical analysis of pottery fragments from Neolithic sites in Poland, dated to around 5500–5000 BC, has provided some of the earliest direct evidence for cheese making in the archaeological record. Researchers identified milk fat residues in perforated ceramic vessels — functionally similar to modern cheese strainers — indicating that Neolithic communities were processing milk by separating curds from whey. This practice would have transformed liquid milk into a more durable, storable food and reduced the lactose content in ways that may have made dairy products more digestible for lactose-intolerant populations.

Cheese making is part of the broader story of secondary products use in Neolithic Europe, wherein communities derived not just meat but also milk, wool, and traction power from their domesticated animals. The ability to preserve dairy in fermented or dried form was of considerable practical value for communities in temperate climates. The Neolithic cultures of Central Europe, including those of present-day Poland, were among the earliest in the world to develop this technology, and their practices spread alongside dairy farming across the continent over the following millennia.
